locked_facts: Minimum speed at top for complete circle: v_top = √(gR)
Minimum speed at bottom: v_bottom = √(5gR) (using energy conservation)
At top: T + mg = mv²/R (both gravity and tension point toward centre)
At bottom: T - mg = mv²/R (tension toward centre, gravity away)
Tension at bottom is maximum, at top is minimum
If v_top < √(gR), the string goes slack and the object falls inward
Tension difference: T_bottom - T_top = 6mg (for a string/rod system)
truth_statements: In vertical circular motion the speed varies — the object is fastest at the bottom and slowest at the top
At the topmost point gravity alone can provide centripetal force if the speed is exactly √(gR)
The minimum speed at the bottom for a complete vertical circle is √(5gR) derived from energy conservation
The tension in the string is maximum at the bottom and minimum at the top of the circle
If the object is on a rigid rod instead of a string it can complete the circle even with zero speed at the top

aha_moment.what_to_show: A ball on a string at three speeds: (1) too slow — string goes slack before reaching the top and ball falls, (2) exactly v = √(5gR) at bottom — ball barely makes it over (T = 0 at top, gravity alone provides centripetal force), (3) fast — comfortable complete circle with tension always positive.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three trajectories: fall-off, critical, and comfortable — with tension arrows at the top
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think any speed works for a complete circle — seeing the critical threshold and the slack string makes the minimum speed condition visceral

simulation_states.label: Forces at different positions
simulation_states.description: Ball moving in vertical circle. At bottom: T points up, mg points down → T - mg = mv²/R. At top: both T and mg point down → T + mg = mv²/R. Tension varies around the loop.
simulation_states.emphasis: At the bottom, tension fights gravity. At the top, tension and gravity both pull inward.

simulation_states.label: Energy conservation
simulation_states.description: Speed is highest at bottom, lowest at top. Energy conservation: ½mv²_bottom = ½mv²_top + mg(2R). Speed at top and bottom related.
simulation_states.emphasis: Energy conservation links top and bottom speeds — the ball slows down as it climbs

simulation_states.label: Critical speed
simulation_states.description: At critical condition: T = 0 at the top → mg = mv²_top/R → v_top = √(gR). Using energy conservation: v_bottom = √(5gR). Below this: string goes slack.
simulation_states.emphasis: v_bottom = √(5gR) is the minimum for a complete circle on a string

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts initial speed at the bottom. Ball traces vertical circle or falls off. Tension displayed at every point. Critical speed highlighted. String vs rod comparison.
simulation_states.emphasis: Set speed below √(5gR) — watch the string go slack! Try a rigid rod — it works even slower.

common_misconceptions.belief: The object needs constant speed for vertical circular motion
common_misconceptions.correction: Speed is NOT constant in vertical circular motion (unless driven by a motor). Gravity decelerates the object going up and accelerates it coming down. Speed is minimum at the top and maximum at the bottom.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show speed bar changing — minimum at top, maximum at bottom

common_misconceptions.belief: If the object has any speed it will complete the circle
common_misconceptions.correction: There is a minimum speed at the bottom: v = √(5gR). Below this, the string goes slack before the top and the object falls inward. The centripetal force requirement at the top sets this critical threshold.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Launch ball too slowly — watch it fail to complete the loop
